title_en: Contracts and Marriage in Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i
content_en:
The commercial and family law sections of Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i demonstrate the full power of al-Kasani's systematic method in the areas where Islamic law is most practically consequential. His conditions-pillars-void/defective distinction framework transforms complex areas of commercial and personal status law into navigable systems.

For the contract of sale, al-Kasani's framework identifies: the conditions for validity (shurut as-sihha) — legal capacity, existing and owned subject matter, deliverability, specified price, absence of conditions that invalidate the contract; the pillars (arkan) — the offer (ijab) and acceptance (qabul); and the invalidating factors that render a contract void (batil, lacking a pillar) or defective (fasid, meeting the pillars but violating a condition). The void/defective (batil/fasid) distinction is among the most important features of Hanafi commercial law and is given particular clarity by al-Kasani's systematic presentation.

The defective (fasid) contract in Hanafi law — valid as to its pillars but invalid as to some condition — occupies a legal space that other schools do not recognize in the same form. Al-Kasani explains that a fasid contract, if executed, transfers ownership but creates a sin and an obligation to cure the defect. This nuanced treatment of defective contracts reflects the Hanafi school's sophistication in commercial law.

The sections on riba in Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i apply al-Kasani's framework to identify which transactions are void and which are merely defective due to riba. Explicit riba — exchange of gold for gold with excess — renders the contract void (batil). Disguised riba through a fasid structure — for example, a sale with a condition that introduces prohibited interest — renders it defective (fasid). This distinction has practical implications for remedies and for the legal treatment of parties who have entered into defective riba transactions.

Marriage in Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i presents the nikah contract's framework: conditions for validity (both parties must be free of impediments — mahram relationships, existing multiple marriages for a man), pillars (offer and acceptance before witnesses), and the factors that make a marriage void versus merely defective. The void marriage (no pillars) versus defective marriage (pillars present but some condition violated) distinction in Hanafi law has significant implications for the legal status of children born and for the parties' rights.

Divorce in Bada'i as-Sana'i presents the talaq's conditions, pillars, and effects systematically. Al-Kasani identifies the conditions for effective talaq (the husband must be adult, sane, and awake — not asleep — when pronouncing it), the forms of talaq (express words vs. implicit words, immediate vs. conditional), and the effects of each form on the 'iddah, revocability, and maintenance obligations.

Al-Kasani's systematic organization of commercial and family law in Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i continues to make the work a valued reference in Hanafi legal education — a model of how systematic organization can enhance both the learnability and the applicability of an extensive legal tradition.
title_id: Kontrak dan Pernikahan dalam Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib asy-Syara'i
content_id:
Bagian-bagian hukum komersial dan keluarga dari Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib asy-Syara'i menunjukkan kekuatan penuh metode sistematis al-Kasani di bidang-bidang di mana hukum Islam paling konsekuensial secara praktis bagi kehidupan sehari-hari. Perlakuannya terhadap jual beli, sewa, kemitraan, dan berbagai jenis kontrak mengikuti kerangka yang sama: kondisi-kondisi untuk kontrak yang sah, rukun-rukun esensial perjanjian, faktor-faktor yang membatalkan kontrak, dan pertanyaan-pertanyaan cabang yang muncul dari kasus-kasus khusus.

Analisis hukum riba sangat rinci dan menangani kelas-kelas barang di mana larangan riba berlaku, kondisi-kondisi khusus yang membuatnya berlaku, dan pertanyaan-pertanyaan tentang kapan pengaturan kontrak yang berbeda menghindari atau melanggar larangan. Perlakuan al-Kasani terhadap riba dalam Bada'i as-Sana'i adalah salah satu yang paling sistematis yang tersedia dalam literatur Hanafi klasik.

Bagian-bagian hukum pernikahan menerapkan kerangka syarat-rukun-pembatal pada institusi pernikahan dengan cara yang mengklarifikasi kondisi-kondisi untuk kontrak pernikahan yang sah, rukun-rukun esensialnya, dan berbagai kejadian — kondisi yang melanggar, perceraian, khulu, dan sebagainya — yang dapat mengakhirinya. Al-Kasani mempresentasikan posisi-posisi Hanafi tentang topik-topik pernikahan yang diperdebatkan — persyaratan wali, persyaratan saksi, status pernikahan sementara — dengan cara yang memperjelas dasar analitis untuk masing-masing. Karya ini tetap menjadi referensi pertama yang dikonsultasikan oleh para sarjana Hanafi yang bekerja pada pertanyaan-pertanyaan hukum keluarga.
content_ar: The commercial and الأسرة law sections of Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i demonstrate the full power of al-Kasani's systematic method in the areas where Islamic law is most practically consequential. His conditions-pillars-void/defective distinction framework transforms complex areas of commercial and personal status law into navigable systems. For the contract of sale, al-Kasani's framework identifies: the conditions for validity (shurut as-sihha) — legal capacity, existing and owned subject matter, deliverability, specified price, absence of conditions that invalidate the contract; the pillars (arkan) — the offer (ijab) and acceptance (qabul); and the invalidating factors that render a contract void (batil, lacking a pillar) or defective (fasid, meeting the pillars but violating a condition). The void/defective (batil/fasid) distinction is among the most المهم features of الحنفي commercial law and is given particular clarity by al-Kasani's systematic presentation. The defective (fasid) contract in الحنفي law — valid as to its pillars but invalid as to some condition — occupies a legal space that other schools do not recognize in the same form. Al-Kasani explains that a fasid contract, إذا executed, transfers ownership but creates a الذنب and an الالتزام to الدواء the defect. This nuanced treatment of defective contracts reflects the الحنفي school's sophistication in commercial law. The sections on riba in Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i apply al-Kasani's framework to identify which transactions are void and which are merely defective بسبب riba. Explicit riba — exchange of gold for gold with excess — renders the contract void (batil). Disguised riba through a fasid structure — على سبيل المثال, a sale with a condition that introduces prohibited interest — renders it defective (fasid). This distinction has practical implications for remedies and for the legal treatment of parties who have entered into defective riba transactions. Marriage in Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i presents the nikah contract's framework: conditions for validity (both parties must be free of impediments — mahram relationships, existing multiple marriages for a man), pillars (offer and acceptance قبل witnesses), and the factors that make a marriage void versus merely defective. The void marriage (no pillars) versus defective marriage (pillars present but some condition violated) distinction in الحنفي law has المهم implications for the legal status of الأولاد born and for the parties' الحقوق. Divorce in Bada'i as-Sana'i presents the talaq's conditions, pillars, and effects systematically. Al-Kasani identifies the conditions for effective talaq (the الزوج must be adult, sane, and awake — not asleep — عندما pronouncing it), the forms of talaq (express words vs. implicit words, immediate vs. conditional), and the effects of each form on the 'iddah, revocability, and maintenance obligations.
